title: Maundy Thursday
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
April 19, 2019 | Preacher: Katie Pearson | Sermon: "Maundy Thursday"

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on Good Friday of Holy Week 2019, the day after Maundy Thursday (April 18). The lectionary readings for Good Friday (Book of Common Prayer/Revised Common Lectionary) would have included Isaiah 52:13-53:12, Psalm 22, Hebrews 10:16-25, and John 18:1-19:42, focusing on Christ's Passion and crucifixion. The church was in the heart of Lent's culmination, moving through the most solemn days of the Christian year, with Easter Sunday following on April 21, 2019.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of the 2018 General Convention (held in Austin, Texas in July 2018), which had made significant decisions including a vote to change marriage canon language to be gender-neutral. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Robert Wright (who served 2013-2023), was an active mid-sized diocese navigating post-Convention implementation. No major episcopal elections or immediate denominational crises were occurring in mid-April 2019, though ongoing tensions around LGBTQ+ inclusion and Anglican Communion relationships remained background context.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver in mid-April 2019 was experiencing typical spring weather patterns with temperatures in the 50s-60s Fahrenheit. The city was focused on the MLB season launch (Colorado Rockies opened their season April 4) and preparing for spring activities. No major local disasters or political upheavals were dominating headlines that week. The Capitol Hill neighborhood where St. John's Cathedral is located was undergoing its typical gentrification-era transformations with ongoing development and demographic shifts.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Mueller Report on Russian interference in the 2016 election had just been released in redacted form on April 18, 2019, dominating political discourse. The Trump administration was dealing with fallout from the report's findings and Democratic calls for further investigation. The 2020 presidential campaign was intensifying, with multiple Democratic candidates declaring and the Iowa caucuses now less than a year away. The National Cathedral in Washington, D.C., had hosted major state funerals and religious services the previous year, keeping Episcopal institutions somewhat prominent in national consciousness.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Notre-Dame de Chartres Cathedral fire in Paris had occurred on April 15, 2019 (just four days before this sermon), devastating the iconic medieval structure and shocking the global Christian community. This catastrophic loss of a major Christian heritage site would have been foremost in the minds of cathedral clergy and congregants. Brexit negotiations in the UK remained chaotic, with Theresa May's government continuing to struggle with parliamentary opposition to various withdrawal agreements. Venezuela's humanitarian crisis was intensifying under Nicolás Maduro's regime, with the Trump administration increasing pressure and recognizing opposition leader Juan Guaidó.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The nation was experiencing a complex moment of religious and institutional anxiety, heightened by the Notre-Dame fire which prompted widespread reflection on heritage, faith, and fragility. "Endgame," the final Avengers film, was about to release (April 26, 2019), dominating popular culture conversation. The cultural moment was one of political polarization, institutional skepticism, and for many Americans, a searching quality around meaning and belonging. For an Episcopal cathedral congregation, there was a particular poignancy to Holy Week 2019—celebrating resurrection and renewal while the world had just watched a 900-year-old sacred structure burn.
